map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.

Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)

map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 foundation, Day 2 Wireless Attack Identifier incidents, Day 3 Scenario 1C clients, Day 4 quiz).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'Best Practices for Public Networks'; the CED scenario is 'Scenario 1C: Verifying Network Authenticity'; the unit hub lists this lesson as 'Public Wi-Fi Security.' All refer to this lesson.

exercise1.title: Wireless Attack Identifier
exercise1.intro: You are a wireless security analyst. Four real incidents just landed on your desk. For each one you will name the CB wireless attack, classify the adversary by skill level, and recommend the CB defense that fits the mechanism. Part A works the incident log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives 1.3.A, 1.3.B, and 1.3.C.
exercise1.narrative: Four wireless incidents were reported across the region this week. Your security operations center logged each one with the location, what observers saw, what the user experienced, and the after-effect. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, find the defining action (SET UP a fake AP, DISRUPT the signal, or DETECT real networks), then match the defense to that mechanism.

rows: A portable router in a corner booth broadcast an identical SSID, 'CoffeeShopGuest,' with a fake login portal.
Connected to the guest network and signed in through the portal as normal.
Days later several customers' social media passwords had been changed.
An unknown device in the parking lot broadcast a high-power signal on the 2.4 GHz band, overwhelming nearby networks.
The video conferencing system kept dropping; no one could stay connected.
Removing the device restored all connections to normal immediately.
A person in a parked car with multiple Wi-Fi antennas and scanning software recorded SSIDs, encryption types, signal strengths, and coordinates.
Nothing — no connection was attempted and no service was interrupted.
A map of every wireless network in range, including where signals leaked outside the building.
A look-alike access point broadcast 'Airport_Free_WiFi'; the real SSID is 'AIRPORT-FREE-Wi-Fi' (different spelling and casing).
Connected and browsed for an hour over HTTPS, including logging into email.
Later the email password was changed and spam was sent from the account.

exercise1.scenarioNote: Incidents adapted from the Day 2 Wireless Attack Identifier debrief; attack names and EKs follow the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: of the four incidents, which one is the trickiest to classify, and what is the single detail that decides it?

partA.q: Incident 1 (coffee shop) and Incident 4 (airport) are both the same CB attack type. Name the attack, quote the defining action in each that proves it, and name the CB defense (with EK) that would have stopped each one BEFORE the credentials were lost.
partA.answer: Both are evil twin attacks (EK 1.3.B.1). Incident 1: the adversary SET UP their own portable router broadcasting an identical SSID with a fake login portal — standing up a look-alike AP is the evil twin signature. Incident 4: the adversary stood up a look-alike AP whose SSID differed only in spelling/casing ('Airport_Free_WiFi' vs. 'AIRPORT-FREE-Wi-Fi'). Defense for both: verify the SSID exactly matches the network you intend to join, character by character, before connecting (EK 1.3.C.3). Verification happens before connection, so it stops the credential capture that occurs at the fake portal; a VPN does not help because the password is handed over at the fake page itself.

partA.q: Classify Incident 2 (shareholder meeting) by its CB attack name, state the broader CB category it belongs to, and explain why NONE of the three EK 1.3.C defenses stop it.
partA.answer: Incident 2 is a jamming attack (EK 1.3.B.2): a high-power EM signal floods the same frequency range, preventing legitimate traffic between the AP and users. CB classifies this explicitly as a denial-of-service (DoS) attack because it blocks access to a resource. The three EK 1.3.C defenses — SSID verification (C.3), VPN (C.1), and protocol-encryption awareness (C.2) — all protect the confidentiality of data; jamming attacks availability, not confidentiality, so none of them apply. The practical response is to switch bands (5 GHz), go wired, or use cellular data.

partA.q: Incident 3 (office parking lot) is frequently misclassified as an evil twin. Name the correct CB attack, explain the one detail that rules out evil twin, and state why individual EK 1.3.C defenses do little against it.
partA.answer: Incident 3 is war driving (EK 1.3.B.3): the adversary detects and maps real wireless network beacons from outside the building — SSIDs, encryption types, signal strengths, coordinates — and finds where the signal leaks beyond the perimeter. The detail that rules out evil twin: nothing fake was set up and no connection was attempted; this is passive reconnaissance of real networks, not impersonation. Individual EK 1.3.C defenses do little because there is no connection for the user to verify or encrypt; the real mitigations are organizational (reduce signal leakage, disable SSID broadcast, use strong WPA3 encryption).

partA.q: Incidents 1 and 4 are both evil twins, yet they differ in adversary skill. Classify each adversary as low-skilled or high-skilled per EK 1.3.A.1 and justify each with a specific detail from the log.
partA.answer: Incident 1 is the work of a low-skilled adversary (EK 1.3.A.1): a pre-built portable router/portal kit can be purchased online and run with no custom code, which matches 'relies on tools created by others.' Incident 4 is high-skilled: deliberately matching the airport's naming convention down to spelling and casing, running the look-alike AP for hours in a major transit hub, and capturing credentials at scale implies custom equipment, knowledge of the venue, and operational stealth — the capacity to build/modify tools and operate beyond an off-the-shelf kit.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: The Incident 4 traveler browsed over HTTPS and was still compromised. Explain why HTTPS did not protect the email login, citing EK 1.3.B.1.
partB.answer: EK 1.3.B.1 says an adversary cannot READ traffic that uses an encrypted protocol like HTTPS — that defeats passive eavesdropping on content. But the traveler typed their password into a fake login page served by the attacker's own access point. HTTPS faithfully encrypted that submission and delivered it straight to the attacker's server; the credentials were handed over, not intercepted. HTTPS stops eavesdropping, not credential harvesting on a fake page.

partB.q: A colleague says 'just use a VPN on public Wi-Fi and you are safe.' Using EK 1.3.C.1, name one thing a VPN does protect against and one thing it does not.
partB.answer: Per EK 1.3.C.1 a VPN encrypts all traffic from your device to the VPN operator's system, so a local attacker on the same Wi-Fi cannot read it — that is real protection against passive local interception. But the VPN provider itself can view the traffic, so a VPN shifts trust rather than eliminating it; and a VPN gives zero protection if you submit credentials to a fake/phishing login page, because you handed the password over willingly.

exercise2.title: Network Trust Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 3 clients - Scenario 1C
exercise2.intro: You are a network security consultant. Three clients need advice on public Wi-Fi. Client 1 is CB Scenario 1C (Verifying Network Authenticity). For each client, name the most likely wireless threat, recommend CB-required defenses BY NAME AND EK, and explain WHY each defense addresses that client's specific risk. Vague 'be careful' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): name the threat + recommend the matching EK 1.3.C defense(s) + tie each to the mechanism, for all 3 clients. Enrichment (optional): name the active-vs-passive sub-type of the attack.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every client
method: Identify the CB wireless attack the client most likely faces (evil twin, jamming/DoS, or war driving) and cite its EK 1.3.B number.
Pick the EK 1.3.C defense(s) that break THIS threat's mechanism: SSID verification (C.3), VPN (C.1), or protocol-encryption awareness (C.2). Layer them when more than one applies.
Explain why the defense addresses this client's exact risk, and state one limitation to warn the client about (e.g., the VPN provider can still view traffic).
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment sub-types (NOT required by the CED for Topic 1.3): Active attacks create or modify traffic (evil twin sets up a fake AP and serves a fake page; jamming floods the band). Passive attacks only observe (war driving detects and maps real beacons; packet sniffing reads traffic). Encryption defeats passive interception; active attacks need verification, availability, or out-of-band defenses.
fields: Limitation to warn the client about:

scenarios.text: CB Scenario 1C — Sunshine Coffee Shop. Jamie connected to 'Guest Wi-Fi' instead of the real 'Sunshine Coffee Wi-Fi.' A fake captive portal asked for a social media login and harvested the credentials. The owner wants to prevent a recurrence AND give customers a safety card.

key.signs: The defining action is a look-alike AP with a near-name SSID plus a fake portal that asked for third-party credentials — Jamie never verified the exact SSID and entered a social media password on an unverified network.
key.mechanism: Attack: evil twin (EK 1.3.B.1). Shop fixes: post the EXACT SSID at the register and each table so customers have a trusted reference (EK 1.3.C.3), and remove any third-party credential login from the real portal (shop Wi-Fi should never ask for a social media password). Customer safety card: verify the SSID (C.3), never enter account passwords into a portal, use a VPN or cellular for sensitive activity (C.1 + C.2).
key.subtype: Active attack — the adversary stands up a fake AP and serves a fake login page (a form of MITM). Trap: VPN is a customer defense, not a shop fix — the shop cannot install VPNs on customers' devices, and credentials are stolen at the portal before any VPN activates.

scenarios.text: Remote Workers — 25 employees work from coffee shops, hotels, and co-working spaces. A senior sales employee's email was compromised at a hotel; forensics suggest credentials were harvested there. No formal public-Wi-Fi policy exists.

key.signs: Hotel is the high-risk venue: guests expect portal logins, network names vary, and time-pressured travelers skip SSID verification — the classic setup for credential capture.
key.mechanism: Likely attack: evil twin (EK 1.3.B.1) in the hotel. Three policy rules, one per CB EK: (1) mandatory company-run VPN for all work activity on non-office Wi-Fi (EK 1.3.C.1); (2) SSID verification against a pre-approved venue list, confirm with staff for new venues (EK 1.3.C.3); (3) high-sensitivity activity (client financials, password changes) on cellular hotspot only (EK 1.3.C.2).
key.subtype: VPN trade-off (EK 1.3.C.1): the VPN provider can VIEW the traffic, so a company-run VPN shifts trust to the EMPLOYER (already authorized for company data) while a consumer VPN trusts a stranger with ALL traffic. Trap: 'use a VPN' without naming WHO you trust drops the points.

scenarios.text: Taylor, High School Senior Traveler — a 17-year-old visiting colleges across 5 cities in 8 days using airport, hotel, coffee shop, and mall Wi-Fi. Activities: personal email, Instagram, Google Docs, Venmo, Netflix, and school email. The phone has cellular data available.

key.signs: Risk depends on the activity, not the venue alone: personal email (credential entry) and Venmo (financial) are HIGH risk; Instagram, Netflix, and Google Docs are LOW-MED because they use HTTPS by default and do not involve risky credential entry.
key.mechanism: HIGH-risk activity (email, Venmo): VPN (EK 1.3.C.1) plus SSID verification (EK 1.3.C.3), or just use cellular data. LOW-MED activity (Instagram, Netflix, Google Docs): HTTPS by default (EK 1.3.C.2), but still verify the SSID first. SSID-verification habit: find the posted SSID, compare spelling/casing/punctuation exactly, and if uncertain, don't connect — use cellular.
key.subtype: Cellular-only situations: Venmo transactions, password changes, and sensitive school email (each tied to data sensitivity per EK 1.3.C.2). Trap: marking Instagram/Netflix as HIGH RISK over-applies the framework — they use HTTPS and don't involve risky credential entry; the skill is knowing WHEN the defense is necessary.

discussion.title: Trusting the Network
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 3)
discussion.overview: We connect to public Wi-Fi using almost no information — usually just a network name and a gut feeling. Adversaries exploit exactly that. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will examine why instinct alone fails on public networks, debate which CB defense is most worth teaching a normal person, and decide what an analyst should and should not conclude when a 'protected' user is still compromised.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. At the coffee shop, the only thing most people use to pick a network is the name on the list. Why do we trust a Wi-Fi network just because its name looks right? What would it actually take to make verifying a network a habit (not just 'pay attention')?
Question 2. Whose job is public-Wi-Fi safety — the person connecting, or the venue that runs the network? Defend your split with one concrete example (e.g., the coffee shop, the airport).
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the Wireless Attack Identifier debrief
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The HTTPS trap  [EK 1.3.B.1]
items.text: The airport victim (Incident 4) used HTTPS and was still compromised. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, explain exactly what HTTPS did protect and what it did NOT protect against in an evil twin attack.

items.label: Task B - Which defense is hardest to live with?  [EK 1.3.C]
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: of the three CED defenses — SSID verification (C.3), VPN (C.1), and protocol-encryption awareness (C.2) — which is hardest for a normal person to actually do every time, and what is the realistic cost of skipping it? Record your group's ranking and one reason.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 3, ~9 min, after the Scenario 1C clients
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'did everything right' case
items.text: A remote worker used a VPN at a hotel and still got their email taken over (Client 2). Design the defense: which single CB action would most directly have stopped this, why did the VPN not save them (EK 1.3.C.1), and when a user who 'used a VPN' is still compromised, what should an analyst conclude — and NOT conclude — about where the credentials were lost?
